text: Take note of those who hold heterodox opinions on the grace of Jesus Christ which has come to us, and see how contrary their opinions are to the mind of God. They abstain from the Eucharist and from prayer, because they confess not the Eucharist to be the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ, which suffered for our sins.
notes: Ignatius equates denial of the real presence with heresy; one of the earliest and clearest patristic attestations.

text: Let that be deemed a proper Eucharist, which is administered either by the bishop, or by one to whom he has entrusted it. Wherever the bishop shall appear, there let the multitude also be; even as, wherever Jesus Christ is, there is the Catholic Church.
notes: First extant use of the phrase "Catholic Church"; directly ties valid Eucharist to the bishop.

text: For not as common bread and common drink do we receive these; but in like manner as Jesus Christ our Saviour, having been made flesh by the Word of God, had both flesh and blood for our salvation, so likewise have we been taught that the food which is blessed by the prayer of His word is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh.
notes: Justin explicitly distinguishes the Eucharistic bread from ordinary food; foundational for real presence doctrine.

text: But every Lord's day do ye gather yourselves together, and break bread, and give thanksgiving after having confessed your transgressions, that your sacrifice may be pure.
notes: Uses thusia (sacrifice) for the Sunday Eucharist; earliest post-apostolic attestation of sacrificial language.

text: He took from among creation that which is bread, and gave thanks, saying, 'This is My body.' The cup likewise, which is from among the creation to which we belong, He confessed to be His blood, and taught the new oblation of the new covenant, offering to God the first-fruits of His own created things.
notes: Irenaeus calls the Eucharist the "new oblation" and links it to Malachi 1:11; key patristic proof for sacrificial interpretation.

text: They were in the habit of meeting on a certain fixed day before it was light, when they sang in alternate verses a hymn to Christ, as to a god.
notes: Non-Christian Roman eyewitness to early Christian worship treating Christ as divine; important external attestation.

text: We do put to confusion all those who assemble in unauthorized meetings, by indicating that tradition derived from the apostles of the very great, the very ancient, and universally known Church founded and organized at Rome by the two most glorious apostles, Peter and Paul.
notes: The locus classicus for apostolic succession; Irenaeus traces Roman episcopal list from Peter.

text: Our apostles also knew, through our Lord Jesus Christ, that there would be strife on account of the office of the episcopate. For this reason they appointed the aforesaid persons and gave instructions that when these should fall asleep, other approved men should succeed them in their ministry.
notes: Earliest post-apostolic text linking church order to apostolic appointment; foundation of succession doctrine.

text: We define that there are two, the Father and the Son, and three with the Holy Spirit, and this number is made by the pattern of salvation... three, however, not in condition, but in degree; not in substance, but in form; not in power, but in aspect.
notes: Tertullian coins the Latin trinitas; first to articulate "three persons, one substance" in the West.

text: Late have I loved you, beauty so old and so new: late have I loved you. And see, you were within and I was in the external world and sought you there.
notes: Augustine's personal testimony of conversion; foundation for the doctrine of prevenient grace.

text: He was made man that we might be made God; and He manifested Himself by a body that we might receive the idea of the unseen Father.
notes: Classic statement of theosis/divinization; encapsulates the soteriological logic of the Incarnation.

text: The episcopate is one, each part of which is held by each one for the whole. The Church also is one, which is spread abroad far and wide into a multitude by an increase of fruitfulness.
notes: Cyprian articulates the unity of the episcopate; foundational text for Catholic ecclesiology.

text: We render thanks to you, O God, through your beloved child Jesus Christ, whom in the last times you sent to us as savior and redeemer and angel of your will... remembering his death and resurrection, we offer to you the bread and the cup.
notes: Earliest complete Eucharistic prayer; explicitly uses sacrificial/offering language ("we offer to you").

text: Together with the Father and the Son the Holy Spirit is to be glorified, because the Spirit too is Lord. Not one of the creatures, not one of those ranked among the served, but one of the royal Trinity.
notes: Basil's definitive defense of the Spirit's divinity; directly influenced the Creed expansion at Constantinople (381).

text: These are the springs of salvation, so that one who thirsts may be satisfied by the words contained in them. In these alone is the good news of the teaching of piety proclaimed. Let no one add to them or take anything from them.
notes: First canonical list matching the modern 27-book New Testament; watershed moment for canon formation.

text: Even infants, who have done nothing good or evil by their own will, are truly baptized for the remission of sins, so that what they have contracted through generation may be cleansed through regeneration.
work_reference: On the Merits and Remission of Sins 1.26
notes: Augustine's clearest statement linking infant baptism to original sin; foundational for Western sacramental theology.

text: The Virgin became a mother while preserving her virginity; she nursed him who gives nourishment to all. He gave milk to Mary as a babe; at the same time he was providing the whole world with his nourishment.
notes: Ephrem's Syriac poetry on Mary's virginal motherhood predates the formal Theotokos definition by decades.

text: I am amazed that there are some who are entirely in doubt as to whether the holy Virgin should be called Theotokos or not. For if our Lord Jesus Christ is God, how is the holy Virgin who gave him birth not Theotokos?
notes: Cyril's argument at Ephesus that denying Theotokos implies Nestorianism; key text for the 431 council.

text: Each nature performs its proper functions in communion with the other; the Word performs what belongs to the Word, the flesh what belongs to the flesh. The one sparkles with miracles, the other succumbs to injuries.
notes: Leo's Tome defined the two-natures Christology ratified at Chalcedon; "Peter has spoken through Leo."

text: The ruler should be a near neighbor to everyone in sympathy, and exalted above all in contemplation, so that through the bowels of loving-kindness he may transfer to himself the infirmities of others.
notes: Gregory's vision of pastoral leadership shaped the medieval episcopate; humility and service as the bishop's model.

text: I do not venerate matter, but I venerate the Creator of matter, who became matter for my sake, who took on the form of matter and through matter accomplished my salvation.
work_reference: Three Treatises on the Divine Images 1.16
notes: John's defense of icon veneration grounded in the Incarnation; vindicated at Second Nicaea (787).
